expanded_description: Perpendicular lines intersect at right angles and create unique geometric relationships. Theorems about perpendicular lines establish connections between perpendicularity and linear pairs, right angles, and parallel lines. The Perpendicular Transversal Theorem and its relatives provide powerful tools for proving angles are right, lines are perpendicular, and relationships among multiple lines in a plane.

core_concept.principle: Perpendicular lines are lines that intersect to form right angles (90°). Key theorems include: If two lines are perpendicular, they form four right angles. If two lines intersect to form one right angle, they form four right angles and are perpendicular. The Perpendicular Transversal Theorem states that if a transversal is perpendicular to one of two parallel lines, it is perpendicular to the other. These theorems connect perpendicularity to the broader framework of angle relationships.
core_concept.explanation: Think of perpendicular lines as the 'squarest' relationship in geometry—they form perfect corners. When lines are perpendicular, every angle at the intersection is exactly 90°. This is a stronger condition than just intersecting; it's a precise, measurable relationship. The Perpendicular Transversal Theorem is like a promise: if you know a line is perpendicular to one parallel rail, it must also be perpendicular to the other rail. This makes sense visually—a vertical post hitting horizontal tracks forms 90° with both. These theorems are the building blocks for rectangles, squares, coordinate axes, and the concept of distance from a point to a line.

image.alt: Three panels: (1) Two perpendicular lines with all four right angles marked. (2) The Perpendicular Transversal Theorem — two parallel lines with a transversal perpendicular to one (marked 90°), and consequently perpendicular to the other. (3) A coordinate plane with perpendicular axes.
image.caption: Perpendicular lines: four right angles at intersection, and the relationship between perpendicular and parallel lines.

sections.section_title: Definition and Basic Theorems of Perpendicular Lines
sections.introduction: Perpendicular lines are defined by the right angle they form. From this definition, several immediate theorems follow: perpendicular lines form four right angles, and if two lines intersect to form one right angle, the lines are perpendicular. These basic theorems establish the logical connection between perpendicularity and angle measure.

introduction_image.alt: Two intersecting lines with one right angle marked (square corner symbol). Arrows show that if one angle is 90°, the adjacent angle is also 90° (linear pair), and by vertical angles, all four are 90°. The perpendicular symbol '⊥' is shown.
introduction_image.caption: If two lines intersect at one right angle, all four angles are right angles and the lines are perpendicular.

key_concept.definition: Perpendicular lines are lines that intersect to form a right angle (90°), denoted by the symbol ⊥ (e.g., m ⊥ n). Theorems: (1) If two lines are perpendicular, they intersect to form four right angles. (2) If two lines intersect to form one right angle, then the lines are perpendicular and form four right angles. (3) If two lines intersect to form a linear pair of congruent angles, then the lines are perpendicular.
key_concept.context: These theorems provide multiple ways to prove lines are perpendicular. You can prove one angle is 90°, or prove adjacent angles are congruent, or prove all four angles are equal. Each path leads to the same conclusion: the lines are perpendicular.

image.alt: A comprehensive diagram: Two perpendicular lines with all four angles marked 90°. Three theorem statements displayed with corresponding diagrams: (1) Perpendicular → 4 right angles. (2) One right angle → perpendicular + 4 right angles. (3) Linear pair of congruent angles → perpendicular.
image.caption: Perpendicular line theorems: multiple ways to establish and use perpendicularity.

process_flow.description: Prove that one angle formed by two intersecting lines measures 90°. This can come from given information, from the definition of perpendicular lines (if given lines are perpendicular), or from algebra.
process_flow.mechanism: Given that lines are perpendicular, m∠1 = 90° by definition. Alternatively, if you can prove m∠1 = 90° through equation solving or angle relationships, you can then conclude the lines are perpendicular.
process_flow.result: One angle at the intersection is known to be 90°.
applications: Given: m ⊥ n → m∠1 = 90° (Definition of Perpendicular Lines).
Given: m∠1 = 90° → m ⊥ n (Definition of Perpendicular Lines).

image.alt: Two intersecting lines with one angle highlighted and labeled 90° with a square corner symbol. An arrow points from the 90° label to the statement 'Lines are perpendicular.'
image.caption: Step 1: Establish that one angle at the intersection is 90°.

process_flow.description: Using the Linear Pair Postulate and Vertical Angles Theorem, prove that all four angles at the intersection measure 90°.
process_flow.mechanism: If m∠1 = 90°, then m∠2 = 180° − 90° = 90° (Linear Pair). ∠3 ≅ ∠1 = 90° and ∠4 ≅ ∠2 = 90° (Vertical Angles). All four angles are 90°. This is used when a proof needs to establish that multiple angles are right angles.
process_flow.result: All four angles at the intersection are right angles (90° each).
applications: m∠1 = 90° → m∠2 = 90° (Linear Pair) → m∠3 = 90° (Vertical to ∠1) → m∠4 = 90° (Vertical to ∠2).
This cascade is often used when proving that a particular angle is right by relating it to a known right angle.

image.alt: Two perpendicular lines with all four angles labeled 90°. Arrows show the logical cascade: ∠1=90° → ∠2=90° (linear pair) → ∠3=90° (vertical to ∠1) → ∠4=90° (vertical to ∠2).
image.caption: Step 2: From one right angle, all four are right by linear pairs and vertical angles.

process_flow.process: Apply the Linear Pair of Congruent Angles Theorem
process_flow.description: If adjacent angles in a linear pair are proven congruent, the lines must be perpendicular. This is an alternative way to prove perpendicularity without measuring 90° directly.
process_flow.mechanism: If ∠1 ≅ ∠2 and they form a linear pair (adjacent angles whose non-common sides form a line), then m∠1 + m∠2 = 180° (Linear Pair Postulate) and m∠1 = m∠2 (congruent). Solving: 2(m∠1) = 180° → m∠1 = 90°. Therefore both are 90° and the lines are perpendicular.
process_flow.result: The lines are proven perpendicular from the congruence of adjacent angles.
applications: Given: ∠1 ≅ ∠2 and they form a linear pair → lines are perpendicular.
This is useful when an angle bisector of a straight angle creates two congruent adjacent angles.

image.alt: Two adjacent congruent angles forming a line. Each marked with matching arcs and 'x'. Equation: x + x = 180° → x = 90°. Conclusion: The lines are perpendicular.
image.caption: Step 3 (Alternative): Congruent angles in a linear pair → each is 90° → perpendicular.

classifications.type: Theorem: Perpendicular Lines Form Four Right Angles
classifications.value: If two lines are perpendicular, then all four angles at the intersection are right angles.
characteristics: Given: m ⊥ n
Conclusion: m∠1 = m∠2 = m∠3 = m∠4 = 90°
Proof: One angle = 90° by definition. Adjacent angle = 90° by Linear Pair. Vertical angles = 90° by Vertical Angles Theorem.
This theorem is used to 'spread' right-angle status to all angles at the intersection.
classifications.behavior: When perpendicular lines are given, this theorem immediately establishes that every angle at the intersection is right. This is frequently the first step after the given in perpendicular line proofs.

examples.process: Using the theorem in a proof
examples.explanation:
Given: AB ⊥ CD at point E.

Conclusion: m∠AEC = 90°, m∠CEB = 90°, m∠BED = 90°, m∠DEA = 90°.

This is the Definition of Perpendicular Lines combined with the theorem.

image.alt: Two perpendicular lines AB and CD intersecting at E. All four angles marked with square corner symbols and 90° labels. The perpendicular symbol AB ⊥ CD shown.
image.caption: Perpendicular lines → four right angles at the intersection.

classifications.type: Theorem: Linear Pair of Congruent Angles Implies Perpendicular
classifications.value: If two lines intersect and a linear pair of angles are congruent, the lines are perpendicular.
characteristics: Given: ∠1 and ∠2 form a linear pair, and ∠1 ≅ ∠2
Conclusion: The lines are perpendicular
Proof: m∠1 + m∠2 = 180° (Linear Pair). m∠1 = m∠2 (congruent). 2(m∠1) = 180° → m∠1 = 90°.
This is an efficient way to prove perpendicularity when congruence of adjacent angles is known.
classifications.behavior: This theorem is a shortcut. Instead of proving an angle equals 90° directly, prove that two adjacent angles are equal, and the algebra forces each to be 90°.

examples.process: Proving perpendicularity from congruent adjacent angles
examples.explanation:
Given: Ray BD bisects ∠ABC, and ∠ABC is a straight angle (A, B, C collinear).

Then ∠ABD ≅ ∠DBC and they form a linear pair.

Conclusion: BD ⊥ AC (the bisector of a straight angle is perpendicular to the line).

image.alt: Points A, B, C collinear (straight angle). Ray BD bisects ∠ABC. ∠ABD and ∠DBC are marked congruent. Since they form a linear pair and are congruent, each must be 90°. BD ⊥ AC.
image.caption: The bisector of a straight angle is perpendicular to the line.

image.alt: Summary diagram: Three pathways to prove perpendicularity — (1) Given as perpendicular, (2) One angle = 90°, (3) Congruent linear pair. Each pathway shown with a diagram and the logical steps to conclude all four angles are 90°.
image.caption: Perpendicular line theorems: multiple pathways to establish and reason about right angles.

sections.introduction: The Perpendicular Transversal Theorem connects perpendicularity to parallel lines. It states that if a transversal is perpendicular to one of two parallel lines, it is also perpendicular to the other. This theorem and its relatives create a powerful logical bridge between the parallel and perpendicular frameworks.

introduction_image.alt: Two parallel lines m and n cut by transversal t. t is perpendicular to m (90° marked). An arrow with a question mark points to the intersection of t and n: 'Is t also perpendicular to n?' The answer is YES, with the angles at n also marked 90°.
introduction_image.caption: If a transversal is perpendicular to one parallel line, must it be perpendicular to the other? Yes—the Perpendicular Transversal Theorem guarantees it.

key_concept.definition: The Perpendicular Transversal Theorem: If a transversal is perpendicular to one of two parallel lines, then it is perpendicular to the other. Equivalently: In a plane, if a line is perpendicular to one of two parallel lines, it is perpendicular to the other.
key_concept.context: This theorem is proved using the Corresponding Angles Postulate. If t ⊥ m at the first intersection, the angle there is 90°. By corresponding angles, the matching angle at the second intersection is also 90°. Therefore t ⊥ n. The proof elegantly connects perpendicularity to the parallel line framework.

image.alt: Proof of the Perpendicular Transversal Theorem: (1) t ⊥ m → ∠1 = 90°. (2) m ∥ n → ∠1 ≅ ∠5 (Corresponding Angles Postulate). (3) m∠5 = 90° (Substitution). (4) t ⊥ n (Definition of Perpendicular Lines). Diagram with all angles labeled.
image.caption: Perpendicular Transversal Theorem proof: perpendicular + parallel → corresponding angles → 90°.

classifications.type: The Perpendicular Transversal Theorem and Its Converse
classifications.value: Both directions of the relationship between perpendicular transversals and parallel lines.
characteristics: Theorem: If a transversal is perpendicular to one of two parallel lines, it is perpendicular to the other.
Converse: In a plane, if two lines are both perpendicular to the same line, then they are parallel to each other.
Proof of Theorem: Corresponding Angles Postulate transfers the 90° from one intersection to the other.
Proof of Converse: Both lines form 90° with the transversal. Corresponding angles are congruent (both 90°), so the lines are parallel.
classifications.behavior: The theorem and its converse together mean: 'perpendicular to the same line' is equivalent to 'parallel' in Euclidean geometry. This is used extensively in coordinate geometry (slopes of perpendicular and parallel lines).

examples.process: Using the converse to prove lines are parallel
examples.explanation:
Given: a ⊥ t and b ⊥ t.

Then m∠1 = 90° and m∠2 = 90° (Definition of Perpendicular).

∠1 ≅ ∠2 (both 90°, Transitive).

∠1 and ∠2 are corresponding angles → a ∥ b (Converse of Corresponding Angles Postulate).

Therefore: Two lines perpendicular to the same line are parallel.

image.alt: Two lines a and b, both perpendicular to transversal t. Both intersections marked 90°. Arrows show the logic: both 90° → corresponding angles congruent → a ∥ b.
image.caption: Converse: Lines perpendicular to the same line are parallel.

worked_examples.title: Perpendicular Transversal Applications
worked_examples.problem:
1) Given: m ∥ n, t ⊥ m. Prove: t ⊥ n.

2) Given: a ⊥ c, b ⊥ c. Prove: a ∥ b.

3) Given: p ∥ q, r ⊥ p, r ⊥ s. Prove: q ∥ s.

step_by_step.explanation:
1) m ∥ n — Given
2) t ⊥ m — Given
3) m∠1 = 90° — Definition of Perpendicular Lines
4) ∠1 ≅ ∠5 — Corresponding Angles Postulate (m ∥ n)
5) m∠5 = 90° — Substitution (or Transitive — replacing ∠1's measure)
6) t ⊥ n — Definition of Perpendicular Lines

step_by_step.step: 2) Two lines perpendicular to the same line
step_by_step.explanation:
1) a ⊥ c — Given
2) b ⊥ c — Given
3) ∠1 is a right angle — Definition (a ⊥ c)
4) ∠2 is a right angle — Definition (b ⊥ c)
5) m∠1 = 90°, m∠2 = 90° — Definition of Right Angle
6) m∠1 = m∠2 — Transitive Property (both = 90°)
7) ∠1 ≅ ∠2 — Definition of Congruent Angles
8) ∠1 and ∠2 are corresponding angles — Definition (relative to a, b, and transversal c)
9) a ∥ b — Converse of Corresponding Angles Postulate

1) p ∥ q — Given
2) r ⊥ p — Given
3) r ⊥ q — Perpendicular Transversal Theorem (from 1 and 2)
4) r ⊥ s — Given
5) q and s are both perpendicular to r — from (3) and (4)
6) q ∥ s — Two lines perpendicular to the same line are parallel (Converse of Perpendicular Transversal)

image.alt: Three diagrams corresponding to the three proofs: (1) Perpendicular Transversal Theorem. (2) Two lines perpendicular to same line → parallel. (3) Chain: p∥q, r⊥p → r⊥q, and r⊥s → q∥s.
image.caption: Perpendicular Transversal Theorem and its applications in multi-step proofs.

worked_examples.final_answer:
See step-by-step for complete proofs.

1) t ⊥ n (Perpendicular Transversal Theorem).
2) a ∥ b (lines perpendicular to same line are parallel).
3) q ∥ s (both perpendicular to r).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Perpendicular Transversal Theorem and its converse connecting perpendicularity and parallelism.

sections.section_title: Distance from a Point to a Line
sections.introduction: The distance from a point to a line is defined as the length of the perpendicular segment from the point to the line. This definition makes the perpendicular segment the shortest path from the point to the line—a fact that has practical consequences in construction, navigation, and optimization.

introduction_image.alt: A line m and a point P not on m. Several segments connect P to various points on m. The perpendicular segment (marked with a square corner) is the shortest. Other segments are visibly longer. Label: 'The perpendicular segment is the shortest distance from a point to a line.'
introduction_image.caption: Distance from a point to a line = length of the perpendicular segment. It is the shortest path.

key_concept.definition: The distance from a point to a line is the length of the perpendicular segment from the point to the line. This perpendicular segment is the unique shortest segment connecting the point to any point on the line. The distance is defined only when the segment is perpendicular to the line.
key_concept.context: This definition is used in triangle geometry (altitude = distance from vertex to opposite side), in coordinate geometry (distance formula for a point to a line), and in optimization problems. It's also the geometric meaning of 'how far' a point is from a line.

image.alt: Point P above line m. Perpendicular segment from P to m meets at point Q (square corner). Length PQ is labeled 'd = distance from P to m.' Other segments from P to other points on m are shown longer, with a note: 'Any other segment is longer than PQ.'
image.caption: Distance = length of perpendicular segment. All other segments are longer.

classifications.value: The perpendicular segment from a point to a line is shorter than any other segment from the point to the line.
characteristics: Given: P is a point not on line m. PQ ⊥ m at Q. R is any other point on m.
Then: PQ < PR (the perpendicular segment is the shortest).
Proof uses the Pythagorean Theorem or triangle inequality: In right triangle PQR, PQ is a leg and PR is the hypotenuse → leg < hypotenuse.
This theorem justifies the definition of distance from a point to a line.
classifications.behavior: This theorem guarantees that the perpendicular segment is the unique minimum distance. Any other path from the point to the line is longer. This is why 'distance' is always measured perpendicularly.

examples.process: Finding distance from a point to a line
examples.explanation:
Given: Point P(3, 4) and line m: y = 0 (the x-axis).

The perpendicular from P to m is vertical, hitting m at (3, 0).

Distance = |4 − 0| = 4 units.

Any other point on m, say (0, 0), gives distance √(3²+4²) = 5 > 4.

image.alt: Coordinate plane: point P(3,4), x-axis as line m. Perpendicular segment to (3,0) length 4. Another segment to (0,0) length 5 shown longer. Right triangle with legs 3 and 4, hypotenuse 5.
image.caption: Perpendicular distance = 4. Any other path is longer (e.g., 5 units).

image.alt: Summary diagram: Definition of distance from point to line, the shortest distance theorem with right triangle proof, and a coordinate geometry example.
image.caption: Distance from a point to a line: definition, the shortest distance theorem, and examples.

sections.section_title: Common Mistakes with Perpendicular Line Theorems
sections.introduction: Perpendicular line proofs require careful attention to definitions and theorem conditions. Common errors include confusing perpendicular with parallel, misapplying the Perpendicular Transversal Theorem when lines are not known to be parallel, and incorrectly assuming two lines are perpendicular based on appearance.

key_concept.definition: Common errors in perpendicular line proofs include: assuming lines are perpendicular because they 'look' perpendicular, applying the Perpendicular Transversal Theorem without establishing that the lines are parallel, confusing perpendicular (⊥, 90°) with parallel (∥), and incorrectly extending perpendicularity through chains without proper justification.
key_concept.context: Perpendicularity must be proven or given—never assumed from a diagram. The Perpendicular Transversal Theorem has a specific hypothesis: one line must be perpendicular and the other two must be parallel. Without both conditions, the theorem does not apply.

image.alt: Four warning panels: (1) Assuming perpendicular from appearance — red X. (2) Using Perpendicular Transversal without parallel lines given — red X. (3) Confusing ⊥ and ∥ symbols — red X. (4) Chaining perpendicularity without proper theorems — red X.
image.caption: Common perpendicular line errors: appearance-based assumptions, missing hypotheses, and symbol confusion.

classifications.value: Treating lines as perpendicular because the diagram makes them look perpendicular.
characteristics: Error: 'The lines look like they meet at 90°, so I'll treat them as perpendicular.'
Correct: Perpendicularity must be given (stated or marked with a square symbol) or proven
Diagrams can be misleading—angles may be 89° or 91° but drawn to look like 90°
classifications.behavior: The only valid indicators of perpendicularity are: (1) explicitly stated in the given, (2) marked with a square corner symbol in the diagram, or (3) proven through logical reasoning from given facts.

examples.explanation:
Valid: 'Given: AB ⊥ CD' — explicit statement.
Valid: A small square at the intersection in the diagram — marked right angle.
Invalid: 'The lines look perpendicular' — appearance is not proof.
Invalid: 'The angle is about 90°' — approximate measurement is not proof.

image.alt: Two diagrams: (1) Lines with a square corner symbol — 'Valid: marked right angle.' (2) Lines that intersect at an angle that looks close to 90° but has no marking — 'Invalid: not marked, not given, not proven.'
image.caption: Perpendicularity requires a given statement, a square mark, or a proof—not appearance.

classifications.note: Solution: Look for the square corner symbol or the ⊥ notation. If neither is present and perpendicularity is not given, you must prove it before using any perpendicular line theorems.

classifications.value: Using the theorem when the lines are not proven to be parallel, or using it in the wrong direction.
characteristics: Error: 't ⊥ m, so t ⊥ n' — without establishing m ∥ n
The theorem requires: (1) m ∥ n, (2) t ⊥ m. Only then can you conclude t ⊥ n.
Both hypotheses must be satisfied before the conclusion applies
classifications.behavior: The Perpendicular Transversal Theorem is a conditional statement with two hypotheses. Both must be verified in the proof. Without parallel lines, a line perpendicular to one line is not necessarily perpendicular to another line.

examples.process: When the theorem does not apply
examples.explanation:
Diagram: Three lines m, n, t. t appears perpendicular to m. n is not parallel to m.

Can we say t ⊥ n? NO. Without m ∥ n, t might intersect n at any angle.

Always check: 'Are the two lines parallel?' before applying the Perpendicular Transversal Theorem.

image.alt: Two diagrams: (1) Correct — m∥n given, t⊥m → t⊥n. (2) Incorrect — m and n are not parallel, t⊥m does NOT imply t⊥n. The second intersection shows an angle clearly not 90°.
image.caption: Without m∥n, the Perpendicular Transversal Theorem does not apply.

classifications.note: Solution: Before using the Perpendicular Transversal Theorem, ask: 'Have I proven (or been given) that the two lines are parallel?' If not, you need to prove parallelism first.

worked_examples.title: Avoiding Perpendicular Line Errors
worked_examples.problem:
Identify the error in each student's reasoning.

1) 'The diagram shows two lines that look like they meet at a right angle, so they are perpendicular.'

2) 'Since p ⊥ q and r ⊥ q, then p ⊥ r.'

3) 'Line t is perpendicular to line m. Therefore, t is perpendicular to line n (which is not parallel to m).'

4) 'The angle is 89°, but that's close enough to 90°, so the lines are perpendicular.'

step_by_step.explanation: Error: Assuming perpendicularity from how the diagram looks. Geometry diagrams are often not drawn to scale. An angle that looks like 90° could be 89° or 91°. Perpendicularity must be given, marked with a square symbol, or proven.

Error: If p ⊥ q and r ⊥ q, the correct conclusion is p ∥ r (two lines perpendicular to the same line are parallel), NOT p ⊥ r. The student confused perpendicular with parallel. Perpendicularity is not transitive—it doesn't chain like parallelism does through a common perpendicular line.

Error: The Perpendicular Transversal Theorem requires the two lines to be parallel. Since m is not parallel to n (or parallelism is not established), the theorem does not apply. t may or may not be perpendicular to n.
Error: Perpendicular lines must form exactly 90° angles. 89° is close but not perpendicular. 'Close enough' is not a valid geometric concept—definitions are exact. The lines are NOT perpendicular.

image.alt: Four panels with the error scenarios diagrammed: (1) Lines without square mark. (2) p⊥q, r⊥q → p∥r, not p⊥r. (3) t⊥m, n not parallel to m → t⊥n not guaranteed. (4) Angle marked 89° with note 'not 90°, not perpendicular.'
image.caption: Correcting perpendicular line errors: valid justifications and precise definitions.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Perpendicularity requires given, marking, or proof, not appearance.
2) p ⊥ q and r ⊥ q implies p ∥ r, NOT p ⊥ r.
3) The Perpendicular Transversal Theorem requires parallel lines; not given.
4) Perpendicular requires exactly 90°, not approximately.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Valid perpendicularity requires exact conditions—given statements, square marks, or proof. Theorems require all hypotheses.

sections.section_title: Practice with Perpendicular Lines and Theorems
sections.introduction: Mastery of perpendicular line theorems requires practice applying definitions, proving perpendicularity, using the Perpendicular Transversal Theorem, and writing complete two-column proofs. Work through these problems to build fluency.

worked_examples.title: Perpendicular Lines Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) Given: m ⊥ n. Prove: m∠1 = 90°, m∠2 = 90°, m∠3 = 90°, m∠4 = 90°.

2) Given: ∠1 ≅ ∠2, and ∠1 and ∠2 form a linear pair. Prove: Lines are perpendicular.

3) Given: a ∥ b, c ⊥ a. Prove: c ⊥ b.

4) Given: p ⊥ t, q ⊥ t. Prove: p ∥ q.

step_by_step.explanation:
1) m ⊥ n — Given
2) m∠1 = 90° — Definition of Perpendicular Lines
3) ∠1 and ∠2 form a linear pair — Definition of intersecting lines
4) m∠1 + m∠2 = 180° — Linear Pair Postulate
5) 90° + m∠2 = 180° — Substitution
6) m∠2 = 90° — Subtraction Property
7) ∠3 ≅ ∠1 (vertical) → m∠3 = 90° — Vertical Angles Theorem + Def. of Congruence
8) ∠4 ≅ ∠2 (vertical) → m∠4 = 90° — Vertical Angles Theorem + Def. of Congruence

1) ∠1 ≅ ∠2 — Given
2) ∠1 and ∠2 form a linear pair — Given
3) m∠1 = m∠2 — Definition of Congruent Angles
4) m∠1 + m∠2 = 180° — Linear Pair Postulate
5) m∠1 + m∠1 = 180° — Substitution
6) 2(m∠1) = 180° — Simplify
7) m∠1 = 90° — Division Property
8) The lines are perpendicular — Definition of Perpendicular Lines

1) a ∥ b — Given
2) c ⊥ a — Given
3) m∠1 = 90° — Definition of Perpendicular Lines (c ⊥ a)
4) ∠1 ≅ ∠5 — Corresponding Angles Postulate (a ∥ b)
5) m∠5 = 90° — Substitution/Transitive
6) c ⊥ b — Definition of Perpendicular Lines

step_by_step.step: 4) Lines perpendicular to same line are parallel
step_by_step.explanation:
1) p ⊥ t — Given
2) q ⊥ t — Given
3) m∠1 = 90° — Definition of Perpendicular (p ⊥ t)
4) m∠2 = 90° — Definition of Perpendicular (q ⊥ t)
5) m∠1 = m∠2 — Transitive (both 90°)
6) ∠1 ≅ ∠2 — Definition of Congruent Angles
7) ∠1 and ∠2 are corresponding angles — Definition (relative to p, q, transversal t)
8) p ∥ q — Converse of Corresponding Angles Postulate

image.alt: Four complete proofs with diagrams: (1) Perpendicular → four right angles. (2) Congruent linear pair → perpendicular. (3) Perpendicular Transversal Theorem. (4) Two lines perpendicular to same line → parallel.
image.caption: Perpendicular lines practice: four key theorems proved in two-column format.

worked_examples.final_answer: See step-by-step for complete two-column proofs with statements and reasons.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Definition of Perpendicular Lines, Linear Pair Postulate, Vertical Angles Theorem, Corresponding Angles Postulate, Perpendicular Transversal Theorem, and their converses.

key_terms: Distance from a Point to a Line
summary: Perpendicular lines intersect at right angles and create unique geometric relationships. Key points: 1) Definition: Perpendicular lines intersect to form right angles (90°). Symbol: m ⊥ n. The definition works both ways: given perpendicular → right angles; given one right angle → perpendicular. 2) Basic Theorems: If two lines are perpendicular, they form four right angles (one given, others by linear pairs and vertical angles). If adjacent angles in a linear pair are congruent, each is 90° and the lines are perpendicular. 3) Perpendicular Transversal Theorem: If a transversal is perpendicular to one of two parallel lines, it is perpendicular to the other. Proof uses the Corresponding Angles Postulate to transfer the 90° measure. 4) Converse: In a plane, two lines perpendicular to the same line are parallel to each other. This is proved using the Converse of the Corresponding Angles Postulate. 5) Distance from a Point to a Line: Defined as the length of the perpendicular segment from the point to the line. This is the shortest distance—any other segment is longer (proved by the Pythagorean Theorem or triangle inequality). 6) Common Errors: Assuming perpendicularity from appearance (must be given, marked, or proven), misapplying the Perpendicular Transversal Theorem without parallel lines, confusing perpendicular (⊥) with parallel (∥), and accepting approximate right angles (must be exactly 90°). 7) These theorems connect perpendicularity to the parallel line framework and are essential for triangle altitude proofs, rectangle/square properties, and coordinate geometry.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Perpendicular Lines' in center. Branches: Definition (intersect at 90°, ⊥ symbol), Basic Theorems (four right angles, congruent linear pair → 90°), Perpendicular Transversal Theorem (⊥ to one parallel → ⊥ to other), Converse (⊥ to same line → parallel), Distance (point to line = perpendicular segment, shortest path), Common Errors (appearance, missing hypotheses, symbol confusion).
review_questions_image.caption: Perpendicular Lines and Theorems Review: definitions, the Perpendicular Transversal Theorem, distance, and connections to parallel lines.
